subject: ap-government
title: Quantitative Analysis — State Elections for Senator and President, 1980–2022
stimulus: Source: Pew Research Center, 2022. Line graph showing the Percentage of Senate Elections Won by Candidates of the Same Party as Their State's Most Recent Presidential Election Winner from 1980 to 2022, illustrating trends in straight-ticket voting.

parts.prompt: Identify the percentage of Senate elections won by candidates of the same party as their state's most recent presidential election winner in 2018, according to the data in the line graph.
parts.scoring_notes: Acceptable answers: 76% is the precise answer, but accept any value between 75% and 80% inclusive.

scoring_points.description: Identify the percentage of Senate elections won by candidates of the same party as their state's most recent presidential election winner in 2018, according to the data in the line graph.

required_elements: 1 pt: Identifies approximately 76% (acceptable range: 75%–80% inclusive) as the percentage of Senate elections won by same-party candidates in 2018.
alternatives.correct_example: 1 pt: Identifies approximately 76% (acceptable range: 75%–80% inclusive) as the percentage of Senate elections won by same-party candidates in 2018.

wrong_examples: About 90%. (Overestimates the 2018 value — the 2018 value is approximately 76%, not near 90%)
The graph shows an increase in 2018. (Describes a trend rather than identifying the specific value — identification requires a number)
common_traps: Read the y-axis value for 2018 specifically — do not report the trend or the value for other years
AP readers accept a reasonable range for line graph readings — any value from 75% to 80% earns the point
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for identifying the percentage of Senate elections won by candidates of the same party as their state's most recent presidential election winner in 2018. Acceptable response: 76%. Scoring notes: identifications between 75% and 80% (inclusive) are also acceptable.

sample_responses.response: In 2018, approximately 76% of Senate elections were won by candidates of the same party as the state's most recent presidential election winner.
sample_responses.commentary: Exact CB value (76%). Earns the point.
Within the 75%–80% acceptable range. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Around 50% of Senate elections were won by same-party candidates in 2018.
sample_responses.commentary: Significantly outside the 75%–80% acceptable range. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Describe the overall trend in the data shown in the line graph.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept any accurate description of the overall increasing trend in same-party voting. The response must describe the overall direction, not just a single data point.

scoring_points.description: Describe the overall trend in the data shown in the line graph.

required_elements: 1 pt: Describes the overall upward trend. Accept: overall, the trend of people choosing a Senate and presidential candidate from the same party is increasing; from 1980 to 2022, voters have shown they are more willing to choose candidates from the same political party in Senate and presidential elections; the percentage of people voting for candidates from different parties (ticket-splitting) has declined.
alternatives.correct_example: 1 pt: Describes the overall upward trend. Accept: overall, the trend of people choosing a Senate and presidential candidate from the same party is increasing; from 1980 to 2022, voters have shown they are more willing to choose candidates from the same political party in Senate and presidential elections; the percentage of people voting for candidates from different parties (ticket-splitting) has declined.

wrong_examples: In 2018, 76% of Senate elections were won by same-party candidates. (Describes a single data point, not a trend — a trend must show direction over time)
The data fluctuates over time. (Acknowledges variation but does not describe the OVERALL directional trend — the dominant feature is an upward trend)
common_traps: There may be fluctuations in the graph, but the OVERALL trend (from start to end) is upward — describe the general direction even if there are dips along the way
The concept underlying this trend is increased partisanship and straight-ticket voting — describing this in plain terms earns the point
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for describing the overall trend in the data shown in the line graph. Acceptable responses: overall, the trend of people choosing a Senate and presidential candidate from the same party is increasing; from 1980 to 2022, voters have shown they are more willing to choose candidates from the same political party in Senate and presidential elections; the percentage of people voting for candidates from different parties has declined.

sample_responses.response: Overall, the percentage of Senate elections won by candidates from the same party as their state's most recent presidential winner has increased from 1980 to 2022, showing a clear upward trend across the 42-year period.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes the overall upward trend across the full time period. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The percentage of voters splitting their tickets between parties in Senate and presidential elections has declined from 1980 to 2022.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes the reciprocal trend (decline in ticket-splitting) which is the same phenomenon. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: In 2018, 76% of Senate elections were won by same-party candidates.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes a single data point rather than an overall trend. A trend must show direction over time. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Draw a conclusion about the level of partisanship among voters since 1980, using the data shown in the line graph.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must draw a conclusion about partisanship — not just describe the trend. The conclusion must be supported by the data.

scoring_points.description: Draw a conclusion about the level of partisanship among voters since 1980, using the data shown in the line graph.

required_elements: 1 pt: Draws a conclusion about partisanship. Accept: partisanship has increased since 1980 as demonstrated by an increase in straight-ticket voting; voters are more likely to vote for Senate and presidential candidates of the same political party over time, demonstrating an increase in partisanship; voters who identify with one party were more likely to vote for candidates of the opposite party in 1980 than they are today.
alternatives.correct_example: 1 pt: Draws a conclusion about partisanship. Accept: partisanship has increased since 1980 as demonstrated by an increase in straight-ticket voting; voters are more likely to vote for Senate and presidential candidates of the same political party over time, demonstrating an increase in partisanship; voters who identify with one party were more likely to vote for candidates of the opposite party in 1980 than they are today.

wrong_examples: More people are voting for the same party in Senate and presidential elections. (Describes the trend without drawing a conclusion about what it means for partisanship)
Partisanship is bad for democracy. (Evaluative claim not grounded in the data — the question asks for a conclusion about the LEVEL of partisanship, not a normative judgment about it)
common_traps: A conclusion must name the concept (partisanship) and make a claim about its direction (increasing) supported by the data (more straight-ticket voting over time)
Do not merely restate the trend — explain what the trend MEANS for partisanship among the electorate
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for drawing a conclusion about the level of partisanship among voters since 1980, using the data shown in the line graph. Acceptable conclusions: partisanship has increased since 1980 as demonstrated by an increase in straight ticket voting; voters are more likely to vote for Senate and presidential candidates of the same political party over time, demonstrating an increase in partisanship; voters who identify with one party were more likely to vote for candidates of the opposite party in 1980 than they are today.

sample_responses.response: Partisanship has increased since 1980 among voters, as demonstrated by the rising share of Senate elections won by candidates of the same party as their state's most recent presidential winner — voters are increasingly aligning their Senate and presidential choices along party lines.
sample_responses.commentary: Names the concept (partisanship), makes a directional claim (increased), and supports it with the specific data. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Voters who identify with one party were more likely to cross over and support candidates from the opposite party in 1980 than they are today — this decline in ticket-splitting demonstrates that partisanship has grown stronger over time.
sample_responses.commentary: Draws the partisanship conclusion by comparing 1980 to today and citing the data as evidence. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: More people vote along party lines now than in 1980.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes the trend without drawing a conclusion specifically about the LEVEL of partisanship among voters. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Explain how the overall trend shown in the line graph could reflect changes in how people acquire political news.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain how a specific change in how people get political news could contribute to the increasing party-line voting trend. Accept any plausible causal connection.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the overall trend shown in the line graph could reflect changes in how people acquire political news.

required_elements: 1 pt: Explains a connection between changes in news acquisition and the trend. Accept: increased demand for ideologically oriented media could have led voters to seek out information reinforcing their political views; the emergence of the internet allowed voters more choices in how they consumed news and made it easier to ignore different perspectives, making them less likely to vote across party lines; social media allows voters to consume the types of news they want rather than a range of perspectives, potentially leading to biases.
alternatives.correct_example: 1 pt: Explains a connection between changes in news acquisition and the trend. Accept: increased demand for ideologically oriented media could have led voters to seek out information reinforcing their political views; the emergence of the internet allowed voters more choices in how they consumed news and made it easier to ignore different perspectives, making them less likely to vote across party lines; social media allows voters to consume the types of news they want rather than a range of perspectives, potentially leading to biases.

wrong_examples: People watch more news today. (Does not explain HOW changes in news consumption would lead to more partisan voting — must describe a causal mechanism)
Social media is divisive. (Too vague — must explain specifically how the way people get news leads to reinforced partisan attitudes and thus more straight-ticket voting)
common_traps: The causal chain is: change in news acquisition (e.g., fragmented media, social media echo chambers) → reinforced partisan identity → more straight-ticket/party-line voting; all three steps do not need to be stated explicitly but the causal connection must be clear

Accept explanations in either direction: ideologically-oriented media causes voters to become more partisan, OR voters who are already more partisan seek out ideologically-aligned media (selection effect)
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how the overall trend shown in the graph could reflect changes in how people acquire political news. Acceptable responses: increased demand for ideologically oriented media could have led voters to seek out information reinforcing their political views; the emergence of the internet allowed voters more choices in how they consumed news and made it easier for them to ignore different perspectives, which made them less likely to vote across party lines; social media allows voters to consume the types of news that they want to see rather than a range of perspectives, potentially leading to biases.

sample_responses.response: The emergence of the internet and cable news allowed voters more choices in how they consumed news and made it easier for them to ignore different perspectives. Over time, voters tuned into ideologically aligned sources that reinforced their existing beliefs, which made them less likely to vote across party lines — driving up the same-party voting shown in the graph.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains a specific change in news acquisition (fragmented internet/cable media) and the causal chain to increased same-party voting. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Social media allows voters to consume the types of news they want to see rather than a range of perspectives, which creates echo chambers. These echo chambers reinforce partisan loyalty and make voters less willing to consider candidates from the opposing party — contributing to the rise in same-party voting in the graph.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies social media and echo chambers as the news-acquisition change and traces the causal mechanism to the observed trend. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: People watch the news on their phones now instead of on TV.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies a change in news consumption but does not explain HOW this change would lead to more partisan voting. No causal chain to the observed trend. 0 pts.
